course_description: Every day we make decisions by relying on our personal theories about how things are supposed to work. But our reliance on these rules is paid for at the cost of accuracy. We tend to see what we expect to see and believe what we're told. One goal of this course is to figure out how everyday decision making can be improved.

Next 2. Aims
course_aims: To cultivate a comprehensive and integrated understanding of judgment and decision-making from a psychological viewpoint. This course aims to enhance students' grasp of how these cognitive processes influence and shape human behaviour, enabling them to apply this knowledge in various real-world contexts. The goal is to equip students with critical thinking skills and a deeper appreciation of the intricacies involved in decision-making, providing a solid foundation for both academic and practical applications in psychology and related fields.

learning_outcomes.description: Understand Core Concepts: Gain a comprehensive understanding of the fundamental theories and concepts in judgement and decision-making, such as biases, heuristics, dual-process theories, and decision-making under uncertainty.

Application to Real-world Scenarios: Analyse and apply judgement and decision-making theories to real-world situations in various domains like medicine, law, and education, enhancing your decision-making abilities and ability to critically evaluate processes.

Interactive Learning with AI: Utilise AI chatbots like 'Coggie' to reinforce your understanding of psychological concepts, identify common decision-making biases, and develop strategies for effective decision-making.

Critical Evaluation of Strategies: Evaluate and analyse various decision-making strategies, including individual and group decision-making processes, the impact of brain training, and mental representations on expertise.

Communication and Persuasion: Effectively communicate complex concepts in judgement and decision-making, persuading others to adopt new approaches based on your understanding developed through interactive assignments and AI tools.

Research Methods and Critical Evaluation: Understand and assess the research methods used to study judgement and decision-making in both laboratory and real-world settings, critically evaluating relevant studies and findings.

Information Synthesis and Presentation: Develop the skill to assess, synthesise, and present information objectively and effectively, drawing from multiple sources to present clear and concise findings.

Argumentation and Debate: Demonstrate the ability to construct and argue for a position on cognitive processes and methods used in psychological research, utilising critical thinking and persuasive communication skills.

AI Literacy and Ethical Considerations: Develop a nuanced understanding of the capabilities and limitations of AI in the context of psychological research and practice, including ethical considerations and the ability to discern reliable from unreliable AI-generated information.

Adaptive Learning and Problem-Solving: Cultivate an adaptive approach to learning and problem-solving, leveraging AI interactions to enhance insight, creativity, and flexibility in addressing complex decision-making scenarios.

assessment_details.title: Weekly In Class Quizzes
assessment_details.task_description:
Every week in class, you’ll take a quiz using traditional bubble sheets and pencils to assess your understanding of the material covered. These weekly quizzes are a crucial part of enhancing your learning and improving retention of the course material. Research indicates that regular testing significantly boosts long-term memory retention compared to merely studying the information (Roediger, Agarwal, McDaniel, McDermott, 2011). These quizzes will challenge you to recall and apply what you’ve learned, helping cement these concepts in your memory.

Each quiz will cover the assigned readings for the week, which may include scientific papers, book chapters, webpages, videos, links, and material from live activities. There are 11 of these quizzes throughout the semester, each contributing 5% to your final grade. To accommodate any off days, we’ll only count your 10 highest scores towards the 50% of your total grade allocated to these quizzes.

Your quiz grades will be available on Blackboard a week or two after each quiz. If you have any concerns about your grades or wish to review a quiz, please reach out to the tutors.

This assessment task evaluates students' abilities, skills and knowledge without the aid of Artificial Intelligence (AI). Students are advised that the use of AI technologies to develop responses is strictly prohibited and may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

assessment_details.title: Weekly Chatbot Interactions with 'Coggie'
assessment_details.task_description:
OBJECTIVE: To deepen your grasp of key concepts, issues, and challenges in judgment and decision-making through direct interaction with 'Coggie', an AI chatbot specifically integrated into the course.

FORMAT: Each week, via a Blackboard link, you will engage with 'Coggie', focusing on the designated topic. These sessions are designed for you to discuss concepts, probe problems, or examine issues related to that week's theme. You're encouraged to ask questions, seek further explanations, explore scenarios, or challenge 'Coggie's responses to foster a deeper understanding.

INTERACTION PROCESS: Weekly, you will submit a detailed log of your interaction with 'Coggie' to Turnitin. This log should reflect the depth and breadth of your engagement, showcasing your exploration and comprehension of the weekly subject matter.

SCORING: Throughout the semester, each interaction offers a chance to earn 2% towards this 20% component of your grade. For the first mark, demonstrate basic but meaningful engagement with 'Coggie'. The second mark is reserved for those who exhibit a higher degree of sophistication and critical thought in their interactions, indicating a deeper and more nuanced understanding.

NOTES:

- Ensure your interactions with 'Coggie' align with the current week's theme. Interactions cannot be carried over or made up in subsequent weeks.

- Logs are due weekly, with specific deadlines stated on Blackboard.

- Given the design of this weekly engagement, extensions or resubmissions are not permitted. Consistency in participation is crucial.

- While AI serves as a tool for exploration and learning, your logs should reflect personal analysis and synthesis. Merely replicating 'Coggie's responses will not meet the task requirements.

- Any misrepresentation of AI-generated content as your own insight, or lack of genuine engagement, may be considered academic misconduct.

- This assessment is structured to be accessible yet intellectually stimulating. It encourages you to engage actively and thoughtfully with the content, using 'Coggie' as a catalyst for learning rather than a substitute for personal understanding.

This revised structure aims to promote continuous, active engagement, offering flexibility and depth in understanding judgment and decision-making through regular, guided AI interaction.

assessment_details.title: AI-Collaborative Concept Exploration & Application
assessment_details.task_description:
In this dynamic project, you will engage with an AI tool like ChatGPT to deeply explore and apply a selected concept from the realm of judgment and decision making. Your task begins by directing the AI to generate initial ideas and perspectives on the chosen concept. You will then critically analyse this AI-generated content, enriching it with your research and insights. The iterative process involves refining and evolving your understanding through subsequent interactions with the AI. Finally, you will reflect on this journey, documenting the evolution of your ideas and the role AI played in this process.

This assignment is designed to enhance your critical thinking and feedback skills, offering a window into the capabilities and limitations of AI as a learning tool. Your performance will be assessed based on the relevance and depth of the chosen concept, the clarity and effectiveness of your interactions with the AI, the quality of your analysis and synthesis, and the insights from your reflective summary. As we progress, more specific guidelines and support will be provided to help you maximise this learning experience.

policies_and_procedures:
University Policies & Guidelines

An overview of the University's assessment-related procedures can be found on my.UQ. ( https://my.uq.edu.au/services/exams-and-assessment )

Academic Integrity

It is the University's task to encourage ethical scholarship and to inform students and staff about the institutional standards of academic behaviour expected of them in learning, teaching and research. Students have a responsibility to maintain the highest standards of academic integrity in their work. Students must not cheat in examinations or other forms of assessment and must ensure they do not plagiarise.

Academic Integrity Modules - Support for Students

It may seem simple – don’t cheat and do reference your work. However, there are many issues involved in understanding what is meant by academic integrity and how you could be misled into engaging in an action which may be academic misconduct. The Academic Integrity Modules (AIM) will enable you to uphold academic integrity throughout your studies at UQ and beyond. From Semester 1, 2021, completion of the modules in your first semester of enrolment will be compulsory for all commencing students.

Plagiarism

The University has adopted the following definition of plagiarism:

Plagiarism is the act of misrepresenting as one's own original work the ideas, interpretations, words or creative works of another. These include published and unpublished documents, designs, music, sounds, images, photographs, computer codes and ideas gained through working in a group. These ideas, interpretations, words or works may be found in print and/or electronic media.

Students are encouraged to read the UQ Student Integrity and Misconduct policy ( http://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.60.04-student-integrity-and-misconduct ) which makes a comprehensive statement about the University's approach to plagiarism, including the approved use of plagiarism detection software, the consequences of plagiarism and the principles associated with preventing plagiarism.

Applications for Extensions

An application for an Extension of Assessment Due Date for medical grounds and/or other exceptional circumstances shall be submitted by lodging the appropriate (online) form with supporting documentation by the date specified in Section 5.3 of the Electronic Course Profile. Students will be notified of the outcome of their request via the myRequests section of my.UQ (and an email sent to their student account).

Additional details associated with extension requests, including acceptable and unacceptable reasons, may be found at my.UQ .

Request for Deferral of Exams

A request for deferral of an exam must be submitted by lodging the appropriate (online) form with supporting documentation via mySI-net > my Requests no later than five (5) calendar days after the date of the original exam. Students are encouraged to familiarise themselves with the information on deferring an exam available on my.UQ .

Feedback on Assessment

There are certain steps you can take if you feel your result does not reflect your performance. Please refer to the my.UQ web site. ( https://my.uq.edu.au/information-and-services/manage-my-program/academic-progress-and-final-results/querying-result )

As a student you have a responsibility to incorporate feedback into your learning; make use of the assessment criteria that you are given; be aware of the rules, policies and other documents related to assessment; and provide teachers with feedback on their assessment practices.

Supplementary assessment

A supplementary assessment is designed to give you a second chance at achieving a passing grade for a course, should you receive a final course grade of 3 (or N subject to the Supplementary Assessment Procedures ). It isn't a repeat of an exam, or a specific assignment – it's designed to test the learning outcomes for the entire course, or specific learning outcomes tailored to you.

Supplementary assessment can take any form (e.g. oral, written, examination etc.), and the highest grade you can receive is a 4 (or P). Supplementary assessment isn't available for every course and is only granted in accordance with the Supplementary Assessment Procedures .

Please check the course profile to see if your course is fully or partially exempt from supplementary assessment. If you have any questions about supplementary assessment, or if you're unsure whether you qualify, please contact staff in your school office or faculty office.

Course grade cut-offs

Any change to grade cut-offs will be in accordance with PPL 3.10.02 Assessment – Procedures .

School of Psychology
Assessment Guidelines

Individual assignments must consist of entirely original material that has been developed solely for the current course. Electronic checks will be conducted to identify instances of plagiarism and collusion.

Every student must complete the University’s Academic Integrity Module (which provides advice regarding plagiarism and ethical assignment behaviour) before completing any assessment. The module can be found at: https://www.uq.edu.au/integrity

Extension Guidelines

The School of Psychology does not guarantee a trouble-free semester of study. The best preparation for unexpected illnesses and other disruptive events is to start your assignments early in the semester. Extensions are not a remedy for students experiencing more significant interruptions to study. Please refer to Section 5.3 of your course profile for specific instructions on applying for extensions or deferred examinations/quizzes. If applying through mysinet for a deferred examination (either mid-semester or end of semester) you must meet the deferred examination eligibility criteria and timelines available at https://my.uq.edu.au/information-and-services/manage-my-program/exams-and-assessment/deferring-exam

Students with chronic conditions are advised to contact a Disability Advisor, UQ Student Services http://www.uq.edu.au/student-services/disability

We strongly encourage students experiencing significant interruptions or chronic illnesses of greater than two weeks to contact UQ Student Services to seek information and/or assistance: http://www.uq.edu.au/student-services/

The School of Psychology uses the following guidelines in the consideration of extension requests. Course coordinators have discretion in deciding whether to grant an extension or not, and so if you are not sure whether you are eligible, please contact your course coordinator.

1. Applications must be lodged by the assignment due date unless there are extenuating circumstances.

2. If the basis of the application is a medical condition, applications should be accompanied by a medical certificate dated prior to the assignment due date, not a statutory declaration.

(a) The illness should be within the period two weeks prior to the advertised due date of the assignment; and

(b) The extension granted should be in proportion to the period of the illness.

3. If the basis of the application is some other circumstance, it must be accompanied by a statutory declaration.

(a) The circumstances should have occurred within the period two weeks prior to the advertised due date of the assignment; and

(b) The extension granted should be in proportion to the period of disruption caused by the circumstances, and typically be no more than two weeks in duration.

4. Students with an approved SAP for the appropriate semester who apply for an extension will be granted 1 week without further documentation provided.

5. Appeals against a refusal of an extension should be lodged through the grievance process outlined on my.UQ https://my.uq.edu.au/services/complaints-and-academic-appeals

6. Students should carefully check the details of their finalised extension or deferred assessment application. This may require students to check in the system for specific days or times for this assessment [eg in SI-Net].

Medical Certificates

It is the student's responsibility to ensure that the provider of a medical certificate is a genuine medical practitioner. If you use an online medical service, please ensure this is a genuine service (many are not). Please note that submission of fake documentation of any kind, even if you are not aware it is fake, constitutes misconduct.

School of Psychology Policy on AI and ChatGPT use in Assessment

The assessments in this course have been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence can provide only limited support and guidance. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI tools. Unless specifically advised by the Course Coordinator students are advised that the use of AI technologies to complete assessment is strictly prohibited and may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

University Policies and Guidelines

Placement Courses

Students on a placement course – also known as a work placement, internship, industry study, industry experience, clinical practice, clinical placement, practical work, practicum, fieldwork, teaching practice – should refer to the University’s Work Integrated Learning and Work Experience Policy ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.10.04-work-integrated-learning-and-work-experience ) for detailed information.

Working with Children

Students whose studies include a professional/work placement, internship, clinical practice, teaching practice or other similar activity which involves them in regular contact with children should refer to the University policy, Working with Children Check - "blue card" ( http://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/1.60.07-working-children ) to find out how to apply for a ‘blue card’.

Students with a Disability

Any student with a disability who may require alternative academic arrangements, including assessment, in the course/program is encouraged to seek advice at the commencement of the semester from a Disability Advisor at Student Services. Refer to the University policy, Students with a Disability (Disability Action Plan) ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.50.08-alternative-academic-arrangements-students-disability ) and to the policy on Special Arrangements for Examinations for Students with a Disability ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.50.09-arrangements-reasonable-adjustments-examinations-students-disability#Procedures ).

While it is the responsibility of the relevant faculty to liaise with professional and registration bodies regarding the acceptability of any adjustment to an academic program, the University Health Service can arrange appropriate advice and assistance on personal and public health issues.

Occupational Health and Safety

Undergraduate and Postgraduate Students should be familiar with the University policies on occupational health and safety in the laboratory ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/2.30.14-occupational-health-and-safety-laboratory ).

Fitness to Practise

Students enrolled in programs and courses that include a practical placement component (also known as work integrated learning, clinical immersion, clinical placement, clinical practice, externship, fieldwork, industry experience, industry study, internship, practicum, teaching practice, work placement) should refer to the University policy Fitness to Practise ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.30.14-fitness-practise ) for detailed information on issues of competence and on management of concerns relating to patient and public safety.

Next Summary
course_introduction:
In PSYC3052: Judgement & Decision Making, you will study how people make decisions and what influences those choices. This course provides a comprehensive understanding of key concepts and theories in the field, including biases and heuristics, dual-process theories, non-human feats of intelligence, reasoning and problem-solving, insight and metacognition, and decision making under uncertainty.

A significant focus of this course is the practical application of these theories to real-world scenarios. You’ll learn to apply judgement and decision-making strategies to situations in fields like medicine, law, and education, and you’ll develop the ability to critically evaluate decision-making processes. Furthermore, you’ll explore advanced topics such as group decision making, the impact of brain training, and the distinctions between fact and fiction. By the end of this course, you’ll be equipped to communicate your understanding of judgement and decision making persuasively and apply these skills in your personal and professional life.

AI IN HIGHER EDUCATION: ENHANCING JUDGMENT AND DECISION MAKING

The rise of artificial intelligence is transforming the university landscape, and in this course, you’ll learn how to use AI tools like ChatGPT effectively. These tools are more than just aids; they’re partners that can help break down complex ideas and improve your grasp of difficult concepts. However, it’s also crucial to understand AI’s limitations, such as its tendency to generate unreliable information or “hallucinations.”

In this course, we will focus on the practical application of AI in enhancing your judgement and decision making skills. You’ll learn to critically evaluate the information provided by AI, understanding when and how it can be a valuable resource and when to rely on your analysis. The goal is to help you become proficient in using these tools to make better decisions, think more critically, and navigate the increasingly digital world with confidence and a discerning eye. By mastering these skills, you’ll be better prepared to use AI as a powerful ally in understanding and applying the principles of judgement and decision making.

COURSE ASSESSMENTS

Your performance in this course will be evaluated through a combination of quizzes, interactions, and projects, totalling 100%:

1. Weekly Quizzes (50%) : Every week, face-to-face quizzes will challenge your understanding of the concepts covered in class. These quizzes are designed to test your grasp of the material and encourage regular, engaged learning.

2. Weekly Chatbot Interactions with 'Coggie' (20%) : Each week, you'll engage with 'Coggie', your AI companion, to explore and apply the specific concepts of that week's theme. These interactive sessions are designed to deepen your understanding of judgement and decision-making in a dynamic, real-time setting. Through dialogue and scenario-based discussions with 'Coggie', you'll enhance your ability to think critically and adaptively, applying the course theories to diverse situations and fostering a more profound comprehension of the decision-making process.

3. AI-Collaborative Concept Exploration and Application (30%) : This comprehensive project allows you to collaborate with AI to explore and apply key course concepts. You’ll start by using AI to generate initial ideas and perspectives, critically analyse and synthesise this content with additional research, and reflect on the process. This task will assess your ability to integrate AI tools into your learning effectively, demonstrating both your understanding of psychological concepts and your critical thinking skills.

NAVIGATING AI USE

While AI will be a significant part of your learning experience, it’s crucial to understand when and how to use it appropriately. Some assessments will require AI as a co-pilot, enhancing your learning and providing a unique perspective on the material. In contrast, other tasks will require unassisted completion to ensure a deep, personal understanding of the concepts. We will provide clear guidelines on the appropriate use of AI for each assessment, ensuring you know when it’s an asset and when it’s essential to rely on your knowledge and reasoning.

Through this course, we aim to prepare you for a world where AI is a ubiquitous presence, equipping you with the skills and knowledge to use this technology responsibly and effectively.

Course Changes in Response to Previous Student Feedback

The course has evolved to integrate enhanced AI interactions, specifically focusing on weekly chatbot engagements and an in-depth “AI-Collaborative Concept Exploration and Application” assignment. This new structure, including regular in-person quizzes and a comprehensive 30% project, is designed to deepen understanding and practical application of course concepts. Students will engage with AI tools in a more consistent and focused manner, enhancing their ability to apply, analyse, and improve upon AI-generated content. These changes aim to bolster critical thinking, understanding, and real-world application of judgment and decision-making theories.

Example criteria for each of the grades can be found in PPL 3.10.02 Assessment Procedures - section 7 Appendix.

Grade X: No assessable work received.

Grade 1, Low Fail: Absence of evidence of achievement of course learning outcomes:

Mark = 0-23.99%

Grade 2, Fail: Minimal evidence of achievement of course learning outcomes:

Mark = 24-46.99%

Grade 3, Marginal Fail: Demonstrated evidence of developing achievement of course learning outcomes:

Mark = 47-49.99%

Grade 4, Pass: Demonstrated evidence of functional achievement of course learning outcomes:

Mark = 50-64.99%

Grade 5, Credit: Demonstrated evidence of proficient achievement of course learning outcomes:

Mark = 65-74.99%

Grade 6, Distinction: Demonstrated evidence of advanced achievement of course learning outcomes:

Mark = 75-84.99%

Grade 7, High Distinction: Demonstrated evidence of exceptional achievement of course learning outcomes:

Mark = 85-100%
late_submission:
Before applying for an extension please read the School of Psychology Assessment Guidelines in Section 6.1 of this Course Profile.

The School of Psychology does not guarantee a trouble-free semester of study. The best preparation for unexpected illnesses and other disruptive events is to start your assignments early in the semester. Extensions are not a remedy for students experiencing more significant interruptions to study.

A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark allocated for the assessment item will be applied for every day or part thereof past the submission deadline, including weekends and public holiday. Assignments submitted more than 7 calendar days after the submission deadline, without an approved extension, will receive 0%.

Type of Assessment
Appropriate Pathway
Link

Assignments

Applications for extension should be submitted before the due date. Students with an approved SAP for the appropriate semester who apply for an extension will be granted 1 week without further documentation provided.

Applications for extension after the due date will only be accepted in exceptional cases (e.g. severe illness, hospitalization, or for compassionate reasons). Please refer to the policy and guidelines (6.1 of this course profile) for further information as well as the following link on my.UQ for extension eligibility https://my.uq.edu.au/information-and-services/manage-my-program/exams-and-assessment/applying-extension
Applying for an assessment extension - my.UQ - University of Queensland

In-Class Quizzes

Only the best 10 out of 11 Quizzes will be counted toward your final grade. If you miss a quiz for medical reasons, your overall mark for quizzes may be reweighed. For example, if you miss one quiz due to illness, it won’t matter because we will end up dropping that quiz because it will be your lowest mark (0 out of 5). If you miss two quizzes due to illness, and you provide adequate documentation, then your final mark will be out of nine quizzes instead of ten (i.e., worth 5.56% each). If you miss three quizzes due to illness, and provide adequate documentation, then your final mark will be out of eight quizzes instead of nine (i.e., worth 6.25% each). Three or more absences will be assessed on a case-by-case basis. Please note that all in-class quizzes must be completed by hand using the provided bubble sheets. No electronic submissions will be accepted.

If you are unable to attempt an in-class quiz at the time specified, please complete a Deferred Exam Request in My SI-Net. Applications must be submitted no later than 5 calendar days after the date of the original quiz. Your supporting documentation should cover the date of the quiz.

Students are required to click on the " Decision Status (Approved or Denied)" link in My SI-Net to determine the outcome of your deferred exam application.

Deferring an exam - my.UQ - University of Queensland

PLEASE SELECT ‘IN-SEMESTER EXAM’ WHEN APPLYING FOR IN-CLASS QUIZZES

Weekly Chatbot Interactions with 'Coggie'

Only the best 10 out of 11 interactions with 'Coggie' will be counted toward your final grade. If you miss an interaction due to medical reasons or other serious causes, and you provide adequate documentation, your overall mark may be reweighed. For example, missing one interaction won't impact your grade, as this will be the one dropped. More than two missed interactions with valid reasons will be assessed on a case-by-case basis to determine the appropriate reweighting of the remaining interactions.

If you are unable to attempt this assessment at the time specified, please complete a Deferred Exam Request in My SI-Net. Applications must be submitted no later than 5 calendar days after the date of the asessment. Your supporting documentation should cover the date of the assessment.

Students are required to click on the " Decision Status (Approved or Denied) " link in My SI-Net to determine the outcome of your application.

other_assessment_information:
Supplementary Assessment

Should you fail a course with a grade of 3, or a non-graded ‘N’, you may be eligible for supplementary assessment. Refer to my.UQ for information on supplementary assessment.

Supplementary assessment may not be available for all courses, or for some of the assessment items for a course. The highest grade you can receive following supplementary assessment is a 4 or P. Details of availability of supplementary assessment for this course are set out below.

Supplementary Assessment is available for this Course .

Refer to my.UQ for how to apply for supplementary assessment.

Supplementary assessment can take any form, for example, an oral or a written exam. Students who are eligible and approved for a supplementary assessment and the form of assessment is an examination, are expected to be available to sit the supplementary exam during the University’s Deferred and Supplementary examination period. Once approved, supplementary assessment cannot be rescinded by the student.

Other
